hardware: Intel Raptor Lake i7, 12P+4E cores, DDR4-3200, ~16 GB RAM, Windows 10

methodology.metric: decode tok/s = sum(step_times[1:]) / (N-1) for ASDSL; llama.cpp CLI footer Generation t/s or eval line
methodology.note: Each stack run standalone (separate processes). No concurrent llama-cli during ASDSL.

phase18_discrepancy.source_b_origin: compare_llama_cpp.py rerun at OMP_NUM_THREADS=13 (see phase18_results e2e_hf_preq_13t); 128-token median ~121ms/token matches 8.24. Overwrote llama_cpp_comparison.json while methodology still listed threads=12 in some fields.
explanation: 11.75 and 11.68/11.29 are the same measurement family (12-thread HF preq, 128 tokens).
8.24 is not a 12-thread result; it matches 13-thread compare and ~97ms engine profile per token.
106% vs llama claim used ASDSL@12t vs llama.cpp CLI@8t (11.1 in stale JSON), not same thread count.
Standalone llama@12t = 13.9 tok/s; ASDSL@12t does not beat llama at equal threads.

llama_bench_tg128_12t.note: llama-bench synthetic tg128, 12 threads — not identical to chat-template CLI

default_configs_stale_json.note: Do not use for parity claims

quality.quality_note: ASDSL HF asymmetric Q4 preq; GGUF Q4_K_M calibrated weights would narrow quality gap

thread_affinity_note: set_thread_count() sets OMP_PLACES={0-7} (8 logical P-cores) even when threads=12; 12 OpenMP threads share 8 places. compare_llama_cpp and phi4_cpu_run both use set_thread_count.

project_summary.honest_assessment: ASDSL matches or beats llama.cpp only when comparing 12-thread ASDSL to 8-thread llama (stale 11.1 figure). At equal threads, llama.cpp is faster (13.9 vs 11.7 tok/s at 12t). ASDSL wins on RAM (~2.4 GB vs ~2.4 GB GGUF) and load time after cache; loses on decode speed and PPL ratio.
